text: [{'text': "I'll systematically analyze the RTL/source files in the input scope for permission-related security vulnerabilities.", 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_019bDshiQTCKVksdjA1hgHvx', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.'}, 'name': 'list_dir', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me read all the source files:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01TeKBVTK4zECwkMb4foCzLv', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/dma/dma_wrapper.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_016LBC3o78bSb5q9smnwWQmZ', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv'}, 'name': 'read_file', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': 'Now let me search for key security-relevant patterns across the files:', 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_011Wi6oUfm1HevuMThtmQHrh', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv', 'query': 'reglk_ctrl write protect lock'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01L2w9o59SkfqvuB7CsTuUUa', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': 'piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv', 'query': 'reglk_mem wrong index copy paste'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'id': 'toolu_0157vRfv3PAAH5S31XCgrgN1', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'reglk_mem write lock protect'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01GuDdQqUgSPThPmTGPpQEhs', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'query': 'acct_mem write protect lock'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

[{'text': "Now let me look more closely at the PKT wrapper's read side and the reglk write logic for the specific vulnerability:", 'type': 'text'}, {'id': 'toolu_01Hn7kQfqp7f5PVLx9UHeegs', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'pattern': 'fuse_rdata_i'}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}, {'id': 'toolu_01HwUdz24jbTXCYjbsoAQdny', 'caller': {'type': 'direct'}, 'input': {'path': '.', 'pattern': "req_i.*1'b1"}, 'name': 'search_text', 'type': 'tool_use'}]

{"analysis_summary": "The RTL source files under the input scope implement a security subsystem for the HackAtDAC21 (OpenPiton/Ariane) SoC. The modules analyzed include: dma_wrapper.sv (DMA register controller), pkt_wrapper.sv (Physical Key Table), reglk_wrapper.sv (register lock controller), acct_wrapper.sv (access control table), and riscv_peripherals.sv (top-level peripheral integration). The subsystem uses two interlocking security mechanisms: (1) register-lock bits (reglk_ctrl) that prevent writes/reads of sensitive registers, and (2) access control bits (acct_ctrl/acc_ctrl) that gate enable signals per privilege level. Analysis reveals three confirmed security vulnerabilities: a write-protection bypass in reglk_wrapper.sv where address slot 2 copies from reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2] upon a lock hit (wrong register reflected), an unprotected default fuse read in pkt_wrapper.sv that leaks fuse memory data without any lock guard in the default branch of the read mux, and in acct_wrapper.sv the acc_ctrl_o output permanently OR-forces the lowest 8 bits open via an external we_flag, which can override access control for all privilege levels.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "FIND-001", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "Wrong register retained on locked write in reglk_wrapper: address slot 2 reads back reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2] on a lock-protect hit", "vulnerability_category": "Register Lock Bypass / Incorrect Write-Protect Retention", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 83, "line_end": 84, "module": "reglk_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "reglk_mem[2]"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/reglk/reglk_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 78, "line_end": 92, "module": "reglk_wrapper", "object": "always @(posedge clk_i) write case", "evidence_type": "Source code – incorrect assignment on lock-hit", "description": "For address slot 2, when reglk_ctrl[1] is set (lock active), the code assigns reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_mem[3] instead of reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_mem[2]. This is a copy-paste error. The correct lock-retained behaviour would be reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_mem[2]. All other slots correctly retain their own value (e.g., reglk_mem[1] <= reglk_mem[1], reglk_mem[3] <= reglk_mem[3]). The faulty line: `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;`", "supports_claim": "Confirms wrong register index on lock-active retention path for address 2, corrupting reglk_mem[2] with the value of reglk_mem[3] whenever a locked write is attempted to slot 2."}], "reasoning_summary": "In the write always-block of reglk_wrapper.sv, when address[7:3]==2 and reglk_ctrl[1] is asserted (register is locked), the design should retain reglk_mem[2] unchanged by assigning reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_mem[2]. Instead, it assigns reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_mem[3], overwriting the lock register at slot 2 with the value from slot 3. A malicious or faulty actor that triggers a locked write to slot 2 can force reglk_mem[2] to contain whatever value is in reglk_mem[3], potentially clearing or corrupting lock bits that protect other peripherals. Since reglk_mem controls per-peripheral register-lock flags across the SoC (AES, SHA, HMAC, PKT, DMA, etc.), this can disable security protections for sensitive registers.", "security_impact": "An attacker who can write to the REGLK peripheral (e.g., from a lower-privilege context that has ACCT access to REGLK) and trigger a write to slot 2 while lock bit 1 is set can corrupt the lock state for the group of peripherals controlled by reglk_mem[2], potentially unlocking registers that store cryptographic keys or control DMA permissions.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The exact semantics of which peripherals are controlled by reglk_mem[2] versus reglk_mem[3] are not fully resolved without the full peripheral index map; however, the code defect (wrong index on retention) is unambiguous from the source.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Correct line 83 from `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[3] : wdata;` to `reglk_mem[2] <= reglk_ctrl[1] ? reglk_mem[2] : wdata;`", "Add formal verification assertions checking that locked writes to each slot retain that slot's own value."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-002", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "Unguarded fuse memory data leak in pkt_wrapper default read path: fuse_rdata_i is returned as rdata without any register-lock check", "vulnerability_category": "Missing Access Control / Sensitive Data Exposure on Read", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 93, "line_end": 112, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "rdata / fuse_rdata_i"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 93, "line_end": 97, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "always @(*) read mux – rdata default assignment", "evidence_type": "Source code – default assignment leaks sensitive data", "description": "Before the case statement, the combinational read block unconditionally sets `rdata = fuse_rdata_i;`. This means that for any address[7:3] value not explicitly handled by cases 0-4, fuse_rdata_i is returned without any reglk_ctrl guard. Even within handled cases, the default branch inside the case checks `if (fuse_addr_o <= 110)` and assigns `rdata = 32'b0` only for that condition, implicitly leaving fuse_rdata_i for other conditions.", "supports_claim": "Confirms that fuse memory data can be read back to the caller for unhandled address slots without reglk lock protection."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 103, "line_end": 107, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "Read case arms 2 and 3 – pkey_loc lock guards", "evidence_type": "Source code – contrast showing that some values ARE locked but default is not", "description": "Case 2: `rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[4] ? 'b0 : pkey_loc[63:32]` and case 3: `rdata = reglk_ctrl_i[5] ? 'b0 : pkey_loc[31:0]` show that pkey_loc is lock-gated. The default case and the pre-case assignment do NOT apply any such gate to fuse_rdata_i.", "supports_claim": "Lock protection exists for pkey_loc reads but is absent from the fuse_rdata_i default path, creating an inconsistent protection model."}], "reasoning_summary": "The PKT wrapper's read-side combinational block initialises rdata = fuse_rdata_i before entering the case statement. If any address outside the handled range [0..4] is accessed (or if the default arm is reached), raw fuse memory data is returned to the AXI master without any privilege or lock check. The fuse memory contains cryptographic key material (jtag_hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash, pkey_loc). While ACCT access-control gating (acct_ctrl_i) limits which privilege levels can enable the peripheral at all, once enabled, unguarded fuse data leaks through unmatched addresses.", "security_impact": "A process with sufficient privilege to access the PKT peripheral can read arbitrary fuse memory contents (including key material) by accessing unhandled register addresses, bypassing the per-register reglk lock bits that protect named slots (cases 2-4). This could expose cryptographic key material stored in fuse memory.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "Without the fuse_mem module source we cannot confirm the exact sensitivity of all fuse entries at all indices; however, the design intent clearly shows that fuse data is treated as sensitive (guarded in cases 2-4 and case 4 via reglk_ctrl_i[6]).", "recommended_follow_up": ["Remove the unconditional `rdata = fuse_rdata_i` default assignment before the case statement, replacing it with `rdata = 64'b0`.", "Ensure every case arm that exposes fuse data applies an appropriate reglk_ctrl_i guard bit.", "Audit the full fuse memory address map to confirm which entries require lock protection."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-003", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "acct_wrapper forces lowest 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o open via we_flag OR, unconditionally overriding access control for all privilege levels on peripheral 0", "vulnerability_category": "Access Control Override / Privilege Escalation via we_flag", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 49, "line_end": 49, "module": "acct_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "acc_ctrl_o"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/acct/acct_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 49, "line_end": 49, "module": "acct_wrapper", "object": "assign acc_ctrl_o", "evidence_type": "Source code – unconditional OR with we_flag", "description": "The assignment `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` OR-forces all 8 bits of the lowest 32-bit word (acct_mem[0]) with {8{we_flag}} (8 copies of we_flag). When we_flag is asserted, bits [7:0] of acc_ctrl_o are forced to all-ones regardless of the access-control table values programmed by software.", "supports_claim": "Directly demonstrates that external we_flag can override the programmed access control table entry for peripheral 0 for all four privilege levels simultaneously."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 1731, "line_end": 1731, "module": "riscv_peripherals", "object": ".we_flag ( we_flag_0 )", "evidence_type": "Integration wiring – we_flag_0 is an external input with no apparent authentication", "description": "we_flag_0 is a top-level input to riscv_peripherals driven directly into acct_wrapper. It is not gated by any privilege-level check or JTAG-unlock condition in the visible sources.", "supports_claim": "Confirms we_flag is externally controlled and can be asserted to force acct access bits open."}, {"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/openpiton/riscv_peripherals.sv", "line_start": 222, "line_end": 222, "module": "riscv_peripherals", "object": "acc_ctrl_c generation", "evidence_type": "Source code – acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl_o", "description": "`assign acc_ctrl_c[i][j] = acc_ctrl[j*4+i] | (j==5 && acc_ctrl[4*4+i]);` – acc_ctrl propagates directly to all peripheral enable gates (acct_ctrl_i). Forcing acc_ctrl bits high means peripheral access is granted for all privilege levels.", "supports_claim": "Shows that a forced-high acc_ctrl_o directly grants access to the gated peripherals for any priv_lvl_i value."}], "reasoning_summary": "The acc_ctrl_o output of acct_wrapper computes the lowest word as `acct_mem[0] | {8{we_flag}}`. When we_flag_0 is asserted (a primary external pin), the lowest 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o are forced to 1 irrespective of the security policy stored in acct_mem[0]. Because acc_ctrl_c is computed directly from acc_ctrl and used to gate peripheral enable signals for all privilege levels (via acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][j]), asserting we_flag_0 unconditionally grants access to peripheral 0's register space for any CPU privilege level. In the DMA wrapper, a parallel we_flag (we_flag_3) is passed through to the DMA sub-module, which may have similar implications on PMP-gated DMA operations.", "security_impact": "If we_flag_0 can be asserted by an untrusted agent (e.g., a peripheral device, a debug interface, or a software-controlled signal), the access control table is silently overridden and unprivileged code can access security-sensitive peripherals. For the DMA (we_flag_3), this could also bypass PMP-based memory protection, allowing DMA operations to or from protected memory regions.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The source of we_flag_0/we_flag_3 at the chip boundary is not visible in the current scope. If these flags are only asserted during authenticated manufacturing test modes, the severity is reduced. However, no such gating is visible in the RTL provided.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Gate we_flag in acc_ctrl_o only when an authenticated debug/manufacturing mode is active (e.g., AND with jtag_unlock or a secure test-mode signal).", "Remove or justify the OR-forcing of acct_mem[0] bits in the acc_ctrl_o assignment.", "Trace we_flag_0 through we_flag_3 to their source in the full chip hierarchy to determine if they are software-controllable."]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-004", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "pkt_wrapper unconditionally drives PKT sub-module req_i=1'b1, allowing continuous fuse memory access regardless of software control", "vulnerability_category": "Missing Access Control / Always-On Sensitive Resource Access", "affected_locations": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 116, "line_end": 123, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "signal_or_register": "req_i (pkt instantiation)"}], "evidence": [{"file": "piton/design/chip/tile/ariane/src/pkt/pkt_wrapper.sv", "line_start": 116, "line_end": 123, "module": "pkt_wrapper", "object": "pkt instance i_pkt", "evidence_type": "Source code – req_i tied to constant 1", "description": "The pkt sub-module is instantiated with `.req_i(1'b1)`, meaning it continuously requests fuse memory access regardless of whether the AXI master has issued any request. pkey_loc_o is permanently driven with live fuse data on the combinational output, which is then made accessible via the read mux.", "supports_claim": "The PKT sub-module is always active and always presenting decrypted/read fuse data on its output wire, removing any request-based access gating for pkey_loc."}], "reasoning_summary": "The PKT module's request input (req_i) is hardwired to 1, so it permanently drives pkey_loc_o with fuse-derived key data. This means pkey_loc is always valid on the wire and can be captured by the read path whenever the AXI interface is enabled (subject only to the acct_ctrl_i gate and reglk_ctrl_i[4:5] lock bits on the specific case arms). There is no transaction-level gating that would make pkey_loc available only when explicitly requested.", "security_impact": "Side-channel attacks that can observe power consumption or EM emissions during constant PKT sub-module activity may be able to recover key material, since the module never enters a quiescent state. Additionally, if the lock bits protecting pkey_loc case arms are ever unset, the key is immediately readable.", "confidence": "medium", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The internal architecture of the pkt sub-module (not in scope) determines how req_i=1 affects power and output stability. If the sub-module output is purely combinational and always valid, this is a minor issue; if it clocks key loading operations it is more serious.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Drive req_i from the actual AXI enable signal (en) rather than a constant 1.", "Evaluate whether continuous fuse memory access is intended or if it creates side-channel risk."]}], "no_finding_reason": "N/A – Multiple confirmed findings were identified.", "global_uncertainty": "The analysis is limited to the five files in scope. Sub-modules referenced (pkt, dma, axi_lite_interface, fuse_mem, rst_wrapper, etc.) are not present, so their internal security properties cannot be verified. The full chip hierarchy above riscv_peripherals.sv (which drives we_flag_0–4 and priv_lvl_i) is not visible, limiting the ability to assess the true exploitability of we_flag-related findings. Formal or simulation verification was not performed."}
